draft.introduction: Cicero’s Tusculan Disputations Book 1 argues that death is not an evil, drawing on Platonic, Stoic, and Epicurean traditions. It represents the Roman philosophical tradition’s most sustained attempt to conquer the fear of death through reason.

translation.text: When I had wholly or at least largely freed myself from legal practice and political duty, I returned, Brutus, chiefly on your encouragement, to those studies which, though stored in memory, had been neglected through circumstance. The whole system and discipline of philosophy, which has been called the culture of the soul, I have always valued most highly.
translation.original_text: Cum defensionum laboribus senatoriisque muneribus aut omnino aut magna ex parte essem aliquando liberatus, rettuli me, Brute, te hortante maxime, ad ea studia

translator_notes.note: Cicero’s phrase cultura animi (culture of the soul) is the origin of the word “culture.” Philosophy is not abstract knowledge but the cultivation and transformation of the self—a Roman restatement of the Greek idea of philosophy as a way of life.
